planting.note: Plant tubers 2 inches deep after the last frost date once soil warms; in zone 7 and south, tubers often overwinter in the ground with a light mulch — lift and store in colder climates.

directions: Hardy begonia is the most cold-tolerant of all begonias, thriving in full to partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil where few other flowering perennials will bloom dependably — it is especially valuable for deep shade gardens in the South. Drooping clusters of soft pink (occasionally white) flowers appear from midsummer through frost on arching, succulent stems with distinctive asymmetrical leaves, providing months of color. Plants produce tiny bulbils in leaf axils that drop to the ground and naturalize freely, gradually forming a lush colony without becoming invasive. In zones 7 and warmer, the tuberous roots survive mild winters underground; top-dress with 2–3 inches of compost each spring to maintain vigor.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Gardening Solutions

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in moist, acidic, organically rich soil in partial to full shade; mulch well to retain moisture and protect the fibrous root system — avoid dry or alkaline soils.

directions: Florida leucothoe is an upright, arching, semi-evergreen to evergreen native shrub that thrives beneath pines and oaks in moist, acidic, sandy or loamy soils — one of the Southeast's most graceful shade-garden shrubs, seldom seen in commerce despite its outstanding ornamental qualities. In spring, fragrant clusters of small white, urn-shaped flowers hang along the arching branches, feeding native bees; glossy, poplar-shaped leaves persist through winter in the Deep South. Plants form loose colonies via underground runners, providing excellent erosion control on shaded slopes and stream banks in the Gulf Coastal Plain from South Carolina to Florida and Mississippi. Grow in consistently moist soil away from drought and reflected heat; pair with native azaleas, swamp roses, and Syngonium for a woodland understory that supports diverse wildlife.

planting.note: Plant small container-grown or balled-and-burlapped trees in fall in well-drained, moist to dry soil; hop hornbeam establishes slowly and resents transplanting of large specimens — start with young nursery stock.

directions: American hop hornbeam is a small to medium understory tree of exceptional adaptability, tolerating everything from deep dry shade under mature oaks to moist bottomland edges — one of the finest native trees for difficult shaded sites where larger trees struggle. Its common name refers to the distinctive hop-like fruiting clusters that hang from branches in summer and fall, providing important food for ruffed grouse, wild turkey, northern bobwhite, and numerous songbirds and small mammals. The wood is extraordinarily hard and dense — historically used for tool handles and fence posts and known as 'ironwood' — and the pale, finely ridged gray bark provides year-round ornamental interest. Grow as a specimen or in groupings beneath taller oaks, hickories, and maples to create a multi-layered native woodland garden; it thrives with minimal care once established and is largely pest and disease free.
sources: USDA Forest Service Silvics of North America

planting.note: Plant in wet to moist, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun to partial shade; buckwheat tree is a wetland obligate native to pond margins, bogs, and bottomland swamps of the Gulf Coast Coastal Plain — keep roots consistently wet.

directions: Buckwheat tree is an evergreen native shrub or small tree of the Gulf Coastal Plain, forming dense thickets in bogs, bay swamps, and along black-water stream margins from South Carolina to eastern Texas — a dominant species in some Gulf Coast wetland plant communities known as 'titi bays.' In early spring, dense racemes of fragrant white to pinkish flowers smother the branches before the leaves fully emerge, providing critical early-season nectar for native bees, including honeybees, which produce a distinctively flavored 'titi honey' prized by beekeepers. The distinctive buckwheat-shaped winged fruits ripen in summer and persist into fall, giving the tree its common name. Grow at the margins of rain gardens, constructed wetlands, or low-lying acidic areas where the soil stays moist to wet — it forms a naturalistic thicket that provides excellent wildlife cover and spring pollinator support.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or sow indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant in spring after frost risk passes — tall ironweed self-seeds freely in moist meadows and stream margins.

directions: Tall ironweed earns its name — plants commonly reach 8 to 10 feet in moist bottomland meadows and woodland edges, bearing brilliant magenta-purple, flat-topped flower clusters in late summer and early fall that stand above neighboring prairie plants and attract a spectacular diversity of butterflies and native bees. As one of the tallest native perennials in the eastern United States, it provides a dramatic vertical accent in native gardens, meadows, and rain garden margins where few other plants achieve its scale while remaining fully native. The intense royal purple flowers are among the most vivid of any North American wildflower — the color is caused by anthocyanin pigments and does not fade even in full southern sun. Cut plants back by half in late June to reduce height and encourage branching if growing in a formal border; allow spent flower heads to stand through winter to feed goldfinches and sparrows.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds on bare soil in fall; seeds require cold stratification to germinate well — fall sowing lets winter temperatures do this naturally; thin to 24-inch spacing in spring once seedlings reach 3–4 inches tall.

directions: Common ironweed is a tough, adaptable native perennial found naturally in moist prairies, roadsides, and streambank edges across the central and eastern United States — its compact size (3–5 feet) compared to tall ironweed makes it more useful in typical garden settings while delivering the same brilliant purple pollinator impact. Dense, flat-topped clusters of vivid purple flowers open from midsummer into fall, overlapping with many other prairie perennials and providing critical late-season nectar for migrating monarchs, swallowtails, and native bumblebees. Plants spread slowly by rhizomes and self-seeding, eventually forming clumps that can be divided every 3–4 years to maintain vigor and share plants. Combine with yellow-flowered natives like sneezeweed, black-eyed Susan, and prairie coneflower for maximum visual impact — the complementary warm yellow and cool purple combination is a classic of native meadow plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow on bare soil in fall for spring germination; seeds benefit from cold stratification; calico aster also spreads readily by self-seeding and can be transplanted as container divisions in spring or fall.

directions: Calico aster is one of the most wildlife-valuable of all native asters, producing enormous quantities of tiny white flowers with magenta-to-purple centers arranged in sprays along horizontal branches — the 'calico' name aptly describes the two-tone disk flowers that change color as they age from yellow to red-purple, creating a patchwork effect. This species is distinguished from similar asters by its tolerance of dry to moist shade under tree canopy, making it one of the few native asters that genuinely thrives in partial woodland shade rather than requiring full sun. Over 70 species of specialist native bees use it as a critical late-season pollen source, and the seeds feed dozens of sparrow, junco, and finch species through fall and winter. Plant in woodland edges, naturalized areas, and native hedgerows where the naturalistic branching form and prolific flowering provide a long season of wildlife habitat.

planting.note: Plant near a fence, shrub, or trellis in moist to wet, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; stems scramble and sprawl through surrounding vegetation rather than twining — provide sturdy support or allow it to clamber through a native shrub border.

directions: Climbing aster is the only vining aster native to North America, an extraordinary scrambling perennial from the Gulf Coastal Plain and Florida that clambers 6–15 feet through shrubs and fences to display enormous masses of small, lavender-to-pink flowers with yellow centers in late fall, often blooming well into December in zone 8 and 9 gardens. Because it flowers so late in the season — October through December in the Deep South — climbing aster serves as a critical last nectar source for late-migrating monarchs and queens, sulphur butterflies, and overwintering native bees that remain active through early winter in the Gulf Coast region. Plant it where it can scramble through a wax myrtle, yaupon holly, or native shrub border; its somewhat weedy scrambling stems are hidden by surrounding plants while the spectacular late-autumn bloom is the garden's last big show before frost. Cut back hard in late winter to control spread and encourage vigorous new stems for next season's bloom.

planting.note: Plant young container-grown trees in fall in wet to seasonally flooded, poorly drained, clay or loamy soil — overcup oak is a bottomland species adapted to extended winter flooding and is one of the best native oaks for wet, compacted, or periodically inundated sites.

directions: Overcup oak is the definitive native tree for chronically wet, flood-prone sites across the Gulf Coastal Plain and Mississippi Alluvial Valley — its name refers to the unusual acorn, where the cup nearly completely encloses the nut, a characteristic unique among North American oaks. Trees are exceptionally flood-tolerant, naturally occurring in swamp forests, seasonally flooded bottomlands, and poorly drained clay flats where other oaks cannot survive periodic inundation, making it the native oak of choice for rain garden margins, detention basins, and low-lying wet areas. Mature trees develop deeply furrowed, blocky gray bark and a broad, spreading crown that provides excellent wildlife value: dozens of species of woodland birds and mammals rely on the mid-sized, nutritious acorns for fall and winter food. Overcup oak grows slowly but lives for centuries — use it as a lasting structural tree in native woodlands, riparian plantings, and large-scale landscape restorations.
sources: USDA Forest Service Silvics of North America

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall in any well-drained soil — honey locust is exceptionally adaptable to alkaline, compacted, salty, wet or dry soils and urban conditions; thornless cultivars ('Inermis', 'Sunburst', 'Skyline') are preferred over the wild thorny species for landscapes.

directions: Honey locust is one of the most broadly adaptable native trees in North America, tolerating alkaline soils, periodic flooding, urban air pollution, road salt, and heat that defeat other trees — widely planted as a tough street and lawn tree in the form of thornless, podless cultivars developed from its natural variety inermis. The feathery, twice-pinnately compound leaves create dappled shade that allows turf and understory plants to grow beneath the canopy more easily than under most large shade trees; the fine-textured leaves also break down quickly in fall without raking. Wild trees produce massive, twisted, dark brown seed pods up to 18 inches long that contain sweet pulp historically eaten by wildlife and indigenous peoples, and formidable branching thorns several inches long; select thornless, seedless cultivars for most landscape uses. Native pollinators, especially short-tongued bees, visit the inconspicuous but very fragrant creamy-white flower clusters heavily in spring, making honey locust an underappreciated urban pollinator plant.
sources: USDA Forest Service Silvics of North America
